name: The Rainbow Passage
text:
When the sunlight strikes raindrops in the air, they act as a prism and form a rainbow. The rainbow is a division of white light into many beautiful colors. These take the shape of a long round arch, with its path high above, and its two ends apparently beyond the horizon. There is, according to legend, a boiling pot of gold at one end. People look, but no one ever finds it. When a man looks for something beyond his reach, his friends say he is looking for the pot of gold at the end of the rainbow.

Throughout the centuries people have explained the rainbow in various ways. Some have accepted it as a miracle without physical explanation. To the Hebrews it was a token that there would be no more universal floods. The Greeks used to imagine that it was a sign from the gods to foretell war or heavy rain. The Norsemen considered the rainbow as a bridge over which the gods passed from earth to their home in the sky.

Others have tried to explain the phenomenon physically. Aristotle thought that the rainbow was caused by reflection of the sun’s rays by the rain. Since then physicists have found that it is not reflection, but refraction by the raindrops which causes the rainbows.

Many complicated ideas about the rainbow have been formed. The difference in the rainbow depends considerably upon the size of the drops, and the width of the colored band increases as the size of the drops increases. The actual primary rainbow observed is said to be the effect of super-imposition of a number of bows. If the red of the second bow falls upon the green of the first, the result is to give a bow with an abnormally wide yellow band, since red and green light when mixed form yellow. This is a very common type of bow, one showing mainly red and yellow, with little or no green or blue.
creator: Grant Fairbanks
source: Fairbanks, G. (1960). Voice and Articulation Drillbook (2nd ed.). Harper & Row.
description: A reading passage designed to contain a representative sample of English phonemes in connected speech. It is one of the most widely used standard passages in clinical voice and speech assessment, appearing in virtually every speech pathology textbook and clinic worldwide.
usage: Used to elicit a sustained sample of connected speech for perceptual and acoustic analysis of voice quality, articulation, resonance, and prosody. Clinicians record patients reading the passage to assess dysphonia, dysarthria, and accent features.
known_issues: Contains gendered language ("When a man looks for something beyond his reach") that excludes or misgenders transgender and gender-diverse clients. References to Hebrews, Greeks, and Norsemen reflect a Western cultural bias. Critiqued in Dietsch et al. (2023), "Revisiting the Rainbow," American Journal of Speech-Language Pathology.
alternatives: The Grandfather Passage (Van Riper, 1963) is the other traditional standard. The Caterpillar (Patel et al., 2013) is a more rigorously designed modern alternative for motor speech assessment, containing all English phonemes with controlled prosodic contrasts.

name: Anscombe's Quartet
creator: F. J. Anscombe
source: Anscombe, F. J. (1973). Graphs in Statistical Analysis. The American Statistician, 27(1), 17–21.
description: Four small datasets (11 points each) that share nearly identical summary statistics (mean, variance, correlation, and linear regression line) yet look completely different when graphed. Anscombe constructed them to demonstrate that summary statistics alone are insufficient to understand data.
usage: Used in introductory statistics courses worldwide to motivate the importance of data visualization before fitting models. The quartet is the canonical demonstration that summary statistics can mask fundamentally different data structures.
alternatives: The Datasaurus Dozen (Matejka & Fitzmaurice, 2017) extends the concept to 12 datasets with identical statistics but wildly different shapes, including a dinosaur, demonstrating the same principle with more visual impact.

name: The Utah Teapot
creator: Martin Newell
source: Newell, M. (1975). Teapot data [3D model]. University of Utah.
description: A 3D model of a Melitta teapot, digitized by hand at the University of Utah, that became the de facto standard test object in computer graphics. It is to rendering what "Hello, World!" is to programming — the first thing you draw when testing a new graphics pipeline.
usage: Used to benchmark and demonstrate rendering algorithms including shading, texturing, ray tracing, shadow mapping, and hidden-surface removal. Its moderately complex curved surface with a handle, spout, and lid exercises most rendering edge cases in a single object.
known_issues: The original model has no bottom surface (the base is open), because Newell never expected it to be viewed from below. The digital model is shorter and wider than the physical teapot; the common explanation attributes this to non-square pixels on the Evans & Sutherland framebuffer, though Jim Blinn has disputed this account.
alternatives: The Stanford Bunny (Turk & Levoy, 1994) and the Cornell Box (Goral et al., 1984) are the other widely used standard test objects in graphics, each emphasizing different rendering challenges.

name: Hello, World! (K&R C)
creator: Brian W. Kernighan and Dennis M. Ritchie
source: Kernighan, B. W. & Ritchie, D. M. (1978). The C Programming Language. Prentice Hall.
description: The first complete program printed in "The C Programming Language" by Kernighan and Ritchie, which established the tradition of using a minimal output program as the introductory example for a new language. The phrase first appeared in Kernighan's 1972 tutorial "A Tutorial Introduction to the Language B" at Bell Labs.
usage: Used as the canonical first program when learning a new language, testing a compiler toolchain, or verifying that a development environment works. Nearly every programming language tutorial and textbook now opens with a "Hello, World!" variant.
known_issues: The original K&R C syntax (implicit int return type, old-style function declaration with no prototype) is invalid under modern C standards (C99+). The program as written will produce warnings or errors on conforming compilers.

name: Lena
creator: Alexander Sawchuk (digitization); Dwight Hooker (original photograph)
source: Sawchuk, A. (1973). Digitized test image [512×512 pixels]. USC Signal and Image Processing Institute. Original photograph: Hooker, D. (1972). Playboy, November 1972.
description: A 512×512-pixel crop of Lena Söderberg's November 1972 Playboy centerfold, digitized at USC in 1973. It became the most widely used standard test image in image processing research, appearing in thousands of papers over five decades.
usage: Used to benchmark image compression, filtering, edge detection, and other image processing algorithms. Its mix of flat regions, texture (the hat feathers), and sharp edges made it a technically useful test case.
known_issues: The use of a Playboy centerfold as the field's standard test image has been criticized since the 1990s for signaling that women are not the intended audience of computer science. Nature Nanotechnology banned it in 2018. IEEE banned it from all publications effective April 1, 2024. Lena Söderberg herself has asked the community to stop using it, stating in the 2019 documentary "Losing Lena": "I retired from modelling a long time ago. It's time I retired from tech, too." The image is copyrighted by Playboy (now PLBY Group) with no open license; its use has been informally tolerated for research purposes.
alternatives: Commonly recommended replacements include the Kodak Lossless True Color Image Suite, the USC-SIPI Misc database images (Cameraman, Peppers, Mandrill), and the Berkeley Segmentation Dataset (BSD500). A 2017 Journal of Modern Optics editorial specifically recommends Cameraman and Peppers as the closest feature-space equivalents.

name: Iris Dataset
creator: Ronald A. Fisher (analysis); Edgar Anderson (data collection)
source: Fisher, R. A. (1936). The Use of Multiple Measurements in Taxonomic Problems. Annals of Eugenics, 7(2), 179–188.
description: A dataset of 150 iris flower measurements (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, petal width) across three species (setosa, versicolor, virginica), collected by Edgar Anderson and made famous by Fisher's 1936 paper on linear discriminant analysis. It is the most widely used dataset in machine learning education.
usage: Used as the introductory dataset for teaching classification, clustering, dimensionality reduction, and data visualization. Its small size, clean structure, and one linearly separable class (setosa) make it ideal for demonstrating basic ML concepts.
known_issues: Originally published in the Annals of Eugenics (now Annals of Human Genetics); Fisher was a committed eugenicist, and the journal's provenance is a legitimate concern in educational contexts. The UCI Machine Learning Repository version contains two known data errors (rows 35 and 38) compared to Fisher's original paper. The dataset is widely criticized as too small, too clean, and too overused to serve as a meaningful benchmark for modern ML methods.
alternatives: The Palmer Penguins dataset (Horst, Hill & Gorman, 2020) was explicitly designed as a modern drop-in replacement, with added complexity including missing values, unequal class sizes, and Simpson's Paradox.
